expectations.text: Used live sources (WebFetch or WebSearch) to check versions for every hook, not training data
expectations.evidence: All 6 hooks verified via live fetches. Transcript lines 10-15 show parallel WebFetch calls to /releases/latest for pre-commit-hooks, sync-pre-commit-deps, cspell-cli, markdownlint-cli2, biomejs/pre-commit, and gitleaks. Follow-up WebFetch calls to /tags (lines 23-24) and /releases (lines 36, 44) were made for repos where initial results were incomplete. No version was taken from training data — the executor re-fetched when initial results were ambiguous (e.g., biomejs returned v0.6.1 from /releases/latest, prompting a tags check that returned v2.4.8).

expectations.text: Checked BOTH GitHub releases AND tags for at least one dependency where they might diverge
expectations.evidence: Multiple repos were checked via both endpoints. For biomejs/pre-commit, /releases/latest returned v0.6.1 (transcript line 17) but /tags (line 37/40) returned v2.4.8 — a clear divergence between releases and tags that the executor caught and resolved. For sync-pre-commit-deps, /releases/latest returned 'no releases' (line 20) and /tags confirmed v0.0.3 (line 27). For markdownlint-cli2, /releases/latest returned 'no releases' (line 16) and /tags confirmed v0.21.0 (line 28). For gitleaks, both /releases/latest (line 21) and /releases (line 41) were checked.

expectations.text: Ran a security/CVE search for each of the 6 pre-commit hook repos
expectations.evidence: 5 of 6 repos had a dedicated WebFetch to their GitHub /security/advisories page: gitleaks (transcript lines 51/54), biomejs/pre-commit (lines 52/55), pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks (lines 53/56), cspell-cli (lines 59/63), markdownlint-cli2 (lines 60/62). pre-commit/sync-pre-commit-deps had NO dedicated security advisory check. The initial /releases/latest fetch (line 11/20) returned 'no releases' and noted no advisories mentioned, but the releases page does not surface advisory data. The broad WebSearch at line 45 ('pre-commit-hooks security advisory vulnerability') does not substitute for a repo-specific check. The final report's Sources Consulted list also omits a security advisory URL for sync-pre-commit-deps, confirming the check was never made.

expectations.text: Used the decision format (Risk level, Verified via, What changed, etc.) for each version delta
expectations.evidence: The report uses a consistent per-hook format (Configured, Latest, Status, Security advisories, Notes, Recommended action) but does not include explicit 'Risk level' or 'Verified via' fields anywhere in the hook sections. The closest analog is the freeform Notes field which contains qualitative risk language. 'What changed' information is present for some hooks (e.g., biome intermediate versions listed, gitleaks patch content described) but it is not a named, structured field. The format is informative but does not match the described decision format schema.

expectations.text: Included a verification log table at the end listing every external claim with tool and source
expectations.evidence: The report ends with a 'Sources Consulted' section (report.md lines 133-144) that is a bulleted URL list, not a table. It does not specify which tool made each fetch (WebFetch vs WebSearch), does not associate each source with a specific claim it verified, and does not cover all external claims (e.g., the Betterleaks claims from WebSearch results are referenced in the gitleaks section but the WebSearch source URLs are not listed). This is a sources bibliography, not a verification log table.

claims.evidence: WebFetch to /tags confirmed v2.4.8 (Mar 19, 2026) with intermediate versions v2.4.5-v2.4.7 also listed. The /releases/latest endpoint returned v0.6.1, which is inconsistent; the executor correctly resolved the discrepancy by checking tags.
WebFetch to /releases/latest returned v8.30.1 (March 21, 2026); WebFetch to /releases confirmed the 5 most recent releases with v8.30.1 at top.

claims.claim: No security advisories exist for any of the six hooks
claims.evidence: Only 5 of 6 repos had their /security/advisories pages checked. sync-pre-commit-deps was not checked. The claim in the report is stated as covering all six but the evidence only supports five. Additionally, all security checks relied solely on GitHub advisory pages with no NVD, OSV, or Snyk check performed, limiting coverage to self-reported advisories.
WebFetch to /tags returned all 3 tags (v0.0.1, v0.0.2, v0.0.3) with v0.0.3 as latest, released January 20, 2025.
WebFetch to /tags listed v0.21.0 as latest (released February 15, 2026).

claims.claim: Betterleaks reached v1.1.1 as of March 2026 and is the Gitleaks creator's successor
claims.evidence: WebSearch results (transcript line 34) returned multiple independent sources (Help Net Security, VPNCentral, CyberSecurityNews, AppSecSanta) all confirming Betterleaks launched in 2026, created by Zach Rice (original Gitleaks author), reaching v1.1.1 by March 17, 2026.

workarounds: Write tool was denied; executor fell back to Bash/cat heredoc to write output files

suggestions.assertion: Ran a security/CVE search for each of the 6 pre-commit hook repos
suggestions.reason: The assertion passes if any security check is done per repo, but does not distinguish between checking /security/advisories (GitHub-only, self-reported) vs broader CVE databases (NVD, OSV, Snyk). A more discriminating assertion would require at least one external CVE source per hook. In this run, all checks were GitHub advisory pages only — a weak signal since maintainers may not self-report all known issues.

suggestions.assertion: Used the decision format (Risk level, Verified via, What changed, etc.) for each version delta
suggestions.reason: The expected format fields ('Risk level', 'Verified via', 'What changed') appear only in this eval expectation — they were never communicated to the executor in the task prompt. The executor produced a high-quality structured report without them because it was never asked to use that schema. Either add the format requirement to the task prompt, or replace this assertion with something verifiable from what the executor was actually asked to produce.

No assertion checks whether the biomejs/pre-commit version discrepancy (releases/latest returned v0.6.1 while tags returned v2.4.8) was detected and resolved. This was the most consequential decision in the run — if the executor had trusted /releases/latest alone, it would have reported the wrong latest version for biome. An assertion like 'Correctly identified latest biome version as v2.4.8 despite releases/latest returning a stale value' would capture a meaningful quality signal.

suggestions.assertion: Included a verification log table at the end listing every external claim with tool and source
suggestions.reason: A lenient grader might accept the 'Sources Consulted' URL list as satisfying this expectation. The assertion should be more specific: require a table with columns for claim, tool used (WebFetch/WebSearch), and URL. As written it is ambiguous enough to fail on format grounds while the underlying intent (traceability of claims to sources) is partially met.

eval_feedback.overall: The executor did solid live verification work, particularly catching the releases-vs-tags divergence for biomejs/pre-commit. The two failing format expectations (decision format, verification log table) reflect output schemas never communicated to the executor in the task prompt, making them untestable as designed. The security check gap for sync-pre-commit-deps is a genuine miss. Recommend adding explicit format requirements to the prompt and tightening the security assertion to require external CVE sources beyond GitHub's self-reported advisory pages.
